differences.title: 1. Architecture
differences.leftDesc: Replication and storage managed by brokers directly. Simpler but less scalable storage.
differences.rightDesc: Decoupled compute and storage. Brokers handle compute, BookKeeper handles storage. Highly scalable.

differences.title: 2. Storage
differences.leftDesc: Relies on broker disks for data persistence. Can be a bottleneck for large data.
differences.rightDesc: Uses Apache BookKeeper for durable, distributed storage. More robust and scalable.

differences.title: 3. Multi-tenancy
differences.leftDesc: Primarily designed for single-tenant or controlled environments. Resource isolation can be challenging.
differences.rightDesc: Native support for isolating tenants with resource quotas and access controls.

differences.title: 4. Geo-replication
differences.leftDesc: Requires additional configurations or tools for replicating data across data centers.
differences.rightDesc: Supports synchronous and asynchronous replication across regions out-of-the-box.

differences.title: 5. Message Consumption
differences.leftDesc: Consumers track their own progress using offsets. Can lead to data loss or duplicates.
differences.rightDesc: Brokers manage cursors for more reliable message acknowledgement and replayability.

differences.title: 6. Topic Management
differences.leftDesc: Partitions are fixed after creation. Resizing requires rebalancing or new topics.
differences.rightDesc: Supports dynamic topic and partition scaling without service interruption.

differences.title: 7. Latency
differences.leftDesc: Optimized for raw throughput and low latency in typical use cases.
differences.rightDesc: Tiered architecture can introduce minimal extra latency, but offers greater scalability.
